metadata.title: AI Video Hallucination Risks for Real Estate Content
metadata.purpose: Catalog known hallucination failure modes in AI-generated video when applied to real estate content. Inform template design and framing choices to minimize risk of generating misleading or obviously artificial property visuals.

risk_categories.description: AI generates impossible or inconsistent architectural elements that do not match the real property. This is the highest-risk category for RE content because viewers (especially Dutch viewers) have strong spatial intuition about buildings.
examples: Extra windows that don't match the real facade
Impossible room proportions (too tall, too narrow)
Doors that lead nowhere or are inconsistent between frames
Staircases with impossible geometry
Roof lines that change between shots
Floor plans that don't match the actual property layout
risk_categories.mitigation: Use close-up framing to limit visible architectural context. Avoid wide exterior shots. Always use real listing photos as reference input. Use inpainting rather than full generation for architectural elements.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Viewers familiar with the property or neighborhood will immediately notice. General viewers may not catch subtle inconsistencies but will notice major ones (floating stairs, impossible windows).

risk_categories.description: When generating multi-shot sequences (e.g., room-to-room walkthrough), AI fails to maintain consistent spatial relationships. A door on the left in one shot appears on the right in the next.
examples: Room dimensions change between shots of the same room
Hallway connects to different rooms in different frames
Window views show different exteriors from the same room
Kitchen counter changes position mid-sequence
Ceiling height shifts between cuts
risk_categories.mitigation: Use single-room clips rather than continuous walkthroughs. Cut between real photos with AI-generated transitions. Avoid long uncut sequences through a property. Each clip should be self-contained.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Even casual viewers notice spatial inconsistencies. This breaks immersion immediately.

risk_categories.description: AI generates materials that shift, shimmer, or morph in unnatural ways. Wood grain that flows like water, marble that changes pattern mid-frame, glass that reflects impossible scenes.
examples: Wood flooring with grain that shifts direction during camera movement
Marble countertops with patterns that morph between frames
Glass reflections showing a scene different from the actual environment
Brick textures that blur or tessellate unnaturally
Metal fixtures that change finish (matte to glossy) mid-shot
risk_categories.mitigation: Keep material close-ups brief (under 2 seconds). Use faster cuts to mask material inconsistency. Avoid slow pans across large surfaces. Apply subtle motion blur in post-production.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Subtle material artifacts are often missed on first watch (especially on mobile at low resolution) but become obvious on re-watch or larger screens.

risk_categories.description: AI adds, removes, or morphs furniture and objects in ways that are unrealistic. Chairs with six legs, tables that float, appliances that blend into walls.
examples: Chairs with extra or missing legs
Tables with impossible proportions
Appliances that partially merge with walls or counters
Decorative objects that change shape or disappear between frames
Light fixtures that defy gravity or lack mounting points
risk_categories.mitigation: Use real staged photos as base. For virtual staging, use specialized staging AI tools (e.g., REimagine, Virtual Staging AI) rather than general-purpose video generators. Review each frame for object consistency before publishing.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Furniture hallucinations are a common 'uncanny valley' trigger. Viewers may not articulate what's wrong but will feel something is off.

risk_categories.description: When AI generates human figures in property videos (e.g., an agent walking through a space), hands, faces, and body proportions frequently distort. Extra fingers, warped faces, impossible body positions.
examples: Extra or missing fingers on a hand touching a door handle
Face distortion when turning toward camera
Body proportions that shift (arms too long, torso compressed)
Clothing that morphs or changes color mid-shot
Hair that defies physics or changes style between frames
risk_categories.mitigation: Avoid generating human figures entirely in AI video. Use real footage of the agent/personality and composite with AI-generated property visuals. If humans must appear, keep them in silhouette, out of focus, or in the background.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Human figure distortion is the single most noticeable AI artifact. Viewers are extremely attuned to human form — even minor distortions are immediately detected and damage credibility.

risk_categories.description: AI generates lighting that is physically impossible — shadows from multiple directions, light sources that don't exist in the scene, time-of-day inconsistencies within a single sequence.
examples: Shadows cast in two different directions in the same room
Sunlight streaming in from a wall with no windows
Interior lighting that changes warmth/intensity between frames
Exterior shots where sun position doesn't match shadow direction
Golden hour lighting on one side of a building and midday on the other
risk_categories.mitigation: Apply consistent color grading in post-production. Use AI video tools that accept lighting direction as an input parameter. Avoid mixing AI-generated exterior and interior shots of the same property in sequence.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Most viewers do not consciously notice lighting inconsistencies. However, professional photographers and videographers will catch them immediately. Low risk for TikTok audiences.

risk_categories.description: AI corrupts any text visible in the scene — street signs, house numbers, agency boards, shop names. Text becomes garbled, misspelled, or nonsensical.
examples: Street name signs with garbled Dutch text
'Te Koop' (For Sale) signs with corrupted letters
House numbers that change between shots
Agency branding on signage that is unreadable
Shop names in the background that are clearly nonsensical
risk_categories.mitigation: Add all text elements in post-production, never rely on AI to generate readable text. Crop or blur any text-bearing elements in the input images. Use overlay templates for branding, pricing, and location information.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Garbled text is one of the most widely recognized AI artifacts. Dutch viewers will immediately spot corrupted Dutch text. Always add text as a post-production overlay.

risk_categories.description: When AI generates wider context around a property (street view, neighborhood, surroundings), it invents buildings, landmarks, and infrastructure that do not exist. This is particularly risky in the Netherlands where city architecture is distinctive and recognizable.
examples: Canal houses with impossible gable shapes not found in that city
Background buildings in a style inconsistent with the neighborhood
Non-existent bridges, canals, or parks visible in exterior shots
Amsterdam-style architecture appearing in a Rotterdam neighborhood
Invented street layouts that don't match the real location
risk_categories.mitigation: Never use AI to generate neighborhood context. Use real drone footage or Google Street View stills for establishing shots. AI generation should be limited to interior scenes and tight exterior framings.
risk_categories.detection_notes: Local viewers will immediately recognize fabricated neighborhood context. Dutch cities have highly distinctive architecture — Amsterdam grachtenpanden, Rotterdam modernism, Utrecht's Dom tower. Faking neighborhood context is extremely high-risk in NL.

examples: Door handle detail
Kitchen faucet
Floor material
Window latch
framing_risk_matrix.notes: Limited context means fewer elements to hallucinate. Single-feature shots have the lowest error rate across all AI video models.

examples: Kitchen view from doorway
Living room from one corner
Bathroom overview
framing_risk_matrix.notes: Furniture layout and proportions can be inconsistent. Material textures on walls and floors may shift. Manageable with careful reference image input and short clip duration.

examples: Open-plan living/kitchen
Room with garden view through windows
framing_risk_matrix.notes: Window views introduce exterior context that AI must hallucinate. The boundary between interior (controllable) and exterior (hallucinated) is a common failure point.
recommended_for: Use with caution. Blur or overexpose window areas.

examples: Front facade with street
Garden view with house
Aerial/drone perspective
framing_risk_matrix.notes: Neighborhood context, sky, landscaping, adjacent buildings, and street infrastructure are all prone to artifacts. Use real footage for exterior establishing shots.
recommended_for: Avoid for AI generation. Use real photos/video.

examples: Overhead property view
Neighborhood fly-over
Approach shot from above
framing_risk_matrix.notes: Maximum context surface area. Every element (rooftops, streets, vegetation, cars, water) must be generated consistently. Current AI video models fail at this scale. Use real drone footage.
recommended_for: Never use AI generation. Always use real drone footage.

overall_guidance.safe_zone: AI video generation is most reliable for: close-up detail shots, text-overlay driven content, single-room medium shots with controlled lighting, and transition effects between real photos.
overall_guidance.danger_zone: AI video generation is least reliable for: wide exterior shots, neighborhood context, continuous multi-room walkthroughs, human figures, and any frame containing readable text.
overall_guidance.recommended_workflow: Use real listing photos and video as the base. Apply AI for: (1) enhancing transitions between real photos, (2) adding cinematic camera movement to static images, (3) virtual staging of empty rooms, (4) atmospheric effects (lighting, time-of-day shifts). Always add text, branding, and pricing as post-production overlays.
overall_guidance.quality_control: Every AI-generated clip must be reviewed frame-by-frame before inclusion in a published video. Flag any frame with visible architectural inconsistency, text corruption, or human figure distortion. A single obviously AI-generated frame can undermine the credibility of an entire video.
